items.foco: Pathophysiology of the lower esophageal sphincter
items.title: LES pathophysiology
items.contentHtml:
Pathophysiology of the LES

  • The lower esophageal sphincter (LES) is an area of ​​high pressure (10-30 mmHg) that prevents reflux; its inappropriate transient relaxation is the predominant mechanism in GERD.
  • Basal hypotony of the LES (<10 mmHg) and intra-abdominal shortening of the esophagus (<1 cm) favor pathological reflux.
  • The angle of His (acute angle between esophagus and stomach) and the phrenoesophageal ligament contribute to anti-reflux competition; Its alteration (e.g. hiatal hernia) predisposes to GERD.
  • Elevated intra-abdominal pressure (obesity, pregnancy) and delayed gastric emptying increase the gastroesophageal gradient.

items.title: Los Angeles Ranking
items.contentHtml:
Los Angeles Classification of Esophagitis

  • Grade A: one or more erosions ≤5 mm in length, non-confluent.
  • Grade B: one or more erosions >5 mm, non-confluent.
  • Grade C: confluent erosions affecting <75% of the circumference esophageal.
  • Grade D: confluent erosions affecting ≥75% of the esophageal circumference.
  • The classification correlates with the severity of acid exposure; grade C/D are predictors of failure to medical treatment and increased risk of complications.

items.title: Indications for endoscopy
items.contentHtml:
Indications for endoscopy in GERD

  • Alarm symptoms: dysphagia, odynophagia, involuntary weight loss, anemia, digestive hemorrhage, epigastric mass.
  • Therapeutic failure after 4-8 weeks of PPI at standard dose.
  • Chronic GERD (>5 years) in men >50 years with risk factors for Barrett's esophagus (obesity, hiatal hernia, nocturnal symptoms).
  • Presurgical evaluation for antireflux surgery, to rule out complications or Barrett's esophagus.
  • Not indicated in typical GERD without warning symptoms or risk factors.

items.title: 24 hour pH-metry
items.contentHtml:
24-hour pH-metry test

  • Gold standard for diagnosis of GERD in patients with typical symptoms without esophagitis (NERD) or with atypical symptoms.
  • Key parameter: percentage of time with pH <4; pathological if >6% in 24 hours.
  • It is measured with a transnasal catheter or wireless capsule (Bravo); The capsule allows greater tolerance and prolonged recording (48-96 hours).
  • Symptom Association Probability (SAP) index >95% or Symptom index (SI) >50% confirm a temporal relationship between reflux and symptoms.
  • Useful to evaluate response to PPI (pH-metry on therapy) and surgical candidacy.

items.title: PPI treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with proton pump inhibitors

  • First line in erosive and non-erosive GERD; standard dose: omeprazole 20 mg, pantoprazole 40 mg, esomeprazole 40 mg once a day, 30-60 min before breakfast.
  • In severe esophagitis (LA C/D) or refractory symptoms: double dose (e.g. omeprazole 20 mg every 12 h) for 8-12 weeks.
  • Do not use PPI as needed in erosive esophagitis; indicate maintenance with the lowest effective dose to prevent relapses.
  • Long-term adverse effects: osteoporosis, vitamin B12 deficiency, Clostridium difficile infection, community-acquired pneumonia; low risk but consider prolonged therapy.

items.title: Chest pain: DDx
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with chest pain

  • First rule out cardiac cause: angina and acute myocardial infarction with ECG, troponins and stress test.
  • GERD produces burning substernal pain, postprandial or nocturnal, which improves with antacids or PPI.
  • Other causes esophageal: diffuse esophageal spasm (pressing pain, intermittent dysphagia, abnormal manometry), achalasia (dysphagia, regurgitation, bird's beak esophagram).
  • Pericarditis: stabbing pain, antalgic position, pericardial rub, diffuse ST elevation.
  • Pain musculoskeletal: reproducible on palpation, unrelated to food.

items.title: Eosinophilic esophagitis vs GERD
items.contentHtml:
Eosinophilic esophagitis vs GERD

  • Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE): dysphagia, food impaction, atopy; biopsy >15 eosinophils/high-power field.
  • GERD: heartburn, regurgitation; biopsy <7 eos/CGA, with response to PPI.
  • PPI test: in EoE there is no complete improvement; pH-metry may show normal acid reflux.
  • EoE is associated with food allergies; treatment: swallowed topical corticosteroids (fluticasone, budesonide) and exclusion diets.
  • Examination trap: do not confuse; EoE can coexist with GERD, but treatment differs.

items.title: Barrett's esophagus
items.contentHtml:
Complications: Barrett's esophagus

  • Intestinal metaplasia in the distal esophagus (columnar epithelium with goblet cells) in response to chronic reflux.
  • Main risk factor: chronic GERD (>5 years), male, >50 years, hiatal hernia, central obesity.
  • Prague classification: circumferential (C) and maximum length (M) of metaplasia; ex: C2M5.
  • Risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma: 0.1-0.5% annually; low-grade dysplasia (LGD) and high-grade dysplasia (HGD) increase the risk.
  • Endoscopic surveillance: no dysplasia every 3-5 years; LGD every 6-12 months; HGD consider endoscopic resection or esophagectomy.

items.title: Atypical and extraesophageal symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Atypical and extraesophageal symptoms

  • Extraesophageal manifestations: chronic cough, posterior laryngitis, asthma, dental erosions, sinusitis, pharyngeal globus.
  • Mechanism: proximal reflux with microaspirations or esophagobronchial vagal reflex.
  • Diagnosis: 24-hour pH-metry with impedanciometry (detects acid and non-acid reflux).
  • Treatment: double-dose PPI for 2-3 months; If there is no response, reevaluate (rule out other causes such as allergy or vocal cord dysfunction).
  • Trap: the response to PPI in extraesophageal symptoms is less predictable than in typical symptoms.

items.title: Bernstein maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Bernstein maneuver for diagnosis

  • Acid perfusion test: alternately infuse saline and 0.1 N hydrochloric acid into the distal esophagus.
  • Positive if the patient reproduces chest pain with acid infusion and not with saline.
  • Sensitivity ~80% and specificity ~90% for GERD; currently out of use due to availability of pH-metry and impedanciometry.
  • Useful in the context of non-cardiac chest pain when other tests are inconclusive.
  • Does not differentiate GERD from eosinophilic esophagitis nor does it quantify acid exposure; It only evaluates esophageal sensitivity to acid.

items.title: Antireflux surgery criteria
items.contentHtml:
Antireflux surgery criteria

  • Main indication: GERD confirmed by pH-metry with partial response or poor tolerance to PPI in the long term.
  • Ideal candidates: large hiatal hernia (>5 cm), LA C/D erosive esophagitis, severe regurgitation, desire to avoid chronic medication.
  • Contraindications: esophageal motility disorder severe (e.g. achalasia, scleroderma), morbid obesity (prefer gastric bypass), advanced age with comorbidity.
  • Mandatory pre-surgical evaluation: endoscopy, 24-hour pH-metry, high-resolution esophageal manometry.
  • Standard procedure: Nissen fundoplication (360°); alternative: Toupet (270°) if esophageal hypomotility.

items.foco: Role of Helicobacter pylori in GERD
items.title: Helicobacter pylori and GERD
items.contentHtml:
Role of Helicobacter pylori in GERD

  • Helicobacter pylori has a dual effect: CagA+ strains reduce reflux by inducing atrophic gastritis (hypochlorhydria), while its eradication can worsen GERD in patients with pangastritis.
  • Routine eradication for GERD alone is not recommended; it is eradicated according to consensus indications (ulcer disease, MALT, risk of gastric cancer).
  • In patients with GERD, eradication does not improve symptoms and may increase the incidence of erosive esophagitis in the short term.
  • The relationship is controversial; There is no evidence to modify the management of GERD based on H. pylori status.
